original_abstract: Neuroscience has historically served as a foundational source of inspiration for artificial intelligence (AI), contributing to breakthroughs in neural network architectures and reinforcement learning. However, as modern AI increasingly diverges from biological principles, it continues to struggle with the robust sensorimotor capabilities and behavioral flexibility observed in even simple animals. We argue that the next generation of AI will require a renewed commitment to NeuroAI—a multidisciplinary field dedicated to distilling the computational principles of the brain. Central to this initiative is the "embodied Turing test," a challenge that task-shifts the benchmark of intelligence from abstract linguistic or strategic performance to the ability of artificial agents to interact with the physical world at a level indistinguishable from living organisms. By focusing on the evolutionary foundations of intelligence—such as efficient motor control and rapid adaptation—researchers can leverage diverse biological datasets to build more capable, energy-efficient, and generalizable systems. Scaling this research will necessitate significant investment in dual-trained scientists, shared high-performance computing platforms, and fundamental research into how integrated neural circuits generate complex behavior. Achieving animal-level sensorimotor intelligence will provide the essential scaffolding for reaching true human-level artificial general intelligence.
humanized_abstract: Artificial intelligence research has long been influenced by findings from the neuroscience community, with neural network and reinforcement learning research being among the many examples where knowledge derived from the brain is used to develop successful approaches to AI. However, the modern approaches to developing complex AI systems seem to be moving away from neuroscience principles and, as a result, these systems still have not yet acquired some of the basic sensorimotor skills and the more complex behavioral adaptations that even relatively simple animals develop rather effortlessly. To progress and eventually develop more sophisticated cognitive abilities in machines, we firmly believe that modern approaches to AI should move toward a new framework that is grounded in NeuroAI — a field that endeavors to extract the computational ideas encoded within the brain. Embodied Turing Test We believe it is high time for a new benchmark, namely an embodied Turing test where the ability of an embodied agent to succeed at its tasks is assessed, independent of whether a human communicator or problem solver is used as a reference, or whether the tasks involve complex thinking or communication. Our goal is to evolve the focus of AI and neuroscience research toward the evolutionally conserved core of cognition, which involves sensorimotor skills, quick responses to changing situations, the evolution of behavioral patterns through learning, efficient control of motor actions, cognitive sensing, and sensorimotion. We also believe that the current diversity of biological samples and associated associated results and data should be taken into account in developing any new computational paradigm. The potential of the approach requires the training of a new set of highly interdisciplinary experts, access to large computing facilities, as well as basic neuroscience research to enable understanding of how the entire brain functions when it is integrated into the controlling agent of a body. Passing the embodied Turing test and developing the full set of animal sensorimotor skills will provide a useful intermediary level of cognition for a machine to build upon in the development of an eventually more advanced AGI system.
